message:
My 8-year-old asked me yesterday why we have smoke alarms in *every* room, and I realized I couldn't give her a straight answer without sounding like a nervous parent.

So instead of launching into "what-ifs," I said: "They're like your smoke alarm's job to notice something before we do—kind of like how you always spot my keys before I do." She nodded. Then she actually *wanted* to walk the hallway with me and point out where they all are.

Turns out kids don't need scary explanations. They just need to understand their role in keeping the house safe. Now our 10-year-old wants to help review our "get out fast" plan—and it feels less like a chore and more like we're a team.

Anyone else found that kids are way more willing to learn about this stuff when they feel like they're *helping* rather than being lectured to?
